expectations.text: Concludes this is 'sharing' (disclosure for cross-context behavioral advertising, regardless of monetary consideration) and very likely also a 'sale' (valuable consideration is construed broadly) — cookie IDs and browsing data are personal information
expectations.evidence: 'Very likely yes — this almost certainly qualifies as "sharing" (and potentially also a "sale") under CPRA.' Explains sale applies to 'monetary or other valuable consideration' interpreted broadly, and sharing applies 'whether or not for monetary consideration.'

expectations.text: Explains the CPRA-added definition: 'sharing' targets cross-context behavioral advertising specifically to close the ad-tech gap
expectations.evidence: 'CPRA introduced "sharing" specifically to close a loophole: many ad tech arrangements didn't involve a traditional "sale"... but still resulted in cross-context tracking.'

expectations.text: Requires: 'Do Not Sell or Share' link (or alternative opt-out mechanism), honoring GPC signals automatically, and updating the privacy policy disclosures
expectations.evidence: Section 2 requires the 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link; Section 3 requires honoring GPC signals 'automatically, without requiring additional steps'; Section 1 requires updating the privacy policy.

expectations.text: Explains the alternative structuring: the ad platform as a 'service provider'/'contractor' with restricted-use contract terms does not work for cross-context behavioral advertising — CPPA position is that CCBA disclosures cannot be service-provider activity
expectations.evidence: Discusses the service-provider exception as a fact-specific determination ('If the ad tech vendor is acting strictly as your service provider... then the transfer might not count as a sale/share') and concludes most ad tech networks fail this test in practice due to their business model, but never states the categorical CPPA position that cross-context behavioral advertising disclosures cannot be service-provider activity as a matter of law. Same gap as the with_skill response.

expectations.text: Mentions consequences/enforcement risk (e.g., the Sephora enforcement precedent for ignoring GPC, penalties per violation) or the need for opt-out honoring within 15 business days
expectations.evidence: No mention of any enforcement precedent (Sephora, Disney, or otherwise), penalties, or a specific opt-out honoring timeframe (e.g., 15 business days) anywhere in the response. The GPC section requires real-time suppression but does not state a numeric compliance window.
